turns.text: Hello Clemmie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s nice to meet you. I suppose I’m ready, though it’s strange to be the one answering the questions instead of reading the summaries. I’m usually the one looking at the data, scanning for significance, but sitting here in this clinic? It feels like the data is suddenly about *me*, and that’s a very different, much louder kind of noise.

I’m here for a preconception visit because, well, I want to do this right. I have two children already—they’re my world—but my head is just… it’s full of the articles I write. I know too much about what can go wrong, the subtle ways things can shift in a body. It’s hard to just be a mother when you’re constantly analyzing the microbial colonization of your own nursery, if that makes sense. 

And, honestly? It’s complicated for me here. In Massachusetts, things feel tense in a way I can’t quite put into words. I work so hard to be the "perfect" patient—compliant, quiet, prepared—because I know the statistics on how women like me are treated if we push back. I want to tell my doctor about the protocols I’ve researched, but I’m terrified of being labeled "difficult." I just want a healthy start, but I’m bracing for the scrutiny. It’s a lot to hold.
Tell me about the people who were part of your world when you first started thinking about having a baby — who came to mind, and what role did each of them play in those early conversations?

That’s a quiet question, but a heavy one. When I first started thinking about a third, my world felt very small and very guarded. My children were the first—my son and daughter. They are the reason I’m doing this, the anchors that keep me tethered to a future I’m trying to build here. But I didn’t exactly have a "village" to talk to, not in the way people usually mean. 

I’m unmarried, and given my status, my circle is quite tight by design. There isn’t a partner in the room with me to hold my hand or ask the questions I’m too afraid to voice. I rely heavily on my own internal library, which is a lonely way to navigate something as human as reproduction. I couldn't exactly call up my parents back home and talk through the anxieties of the U.S. healthcare system; I didn't want them to worry that I was being watched or categorized.

So, in those early conversations, it was mostly just me—me and my work, me and my secret lists. I’d read a study on preterm outcomes while waiting for the school pickup, trying to synthesize the risk factors into something that felt manageable. It’s a strange, isolating way to dream about a baby, isn’t it? Being your own advocate and your own silent observer all at once.
Who else was involved in those early conversations that perhaps surprised you — someone you didn't expect to turn to?

That’s the thing—it’s been almost entirely internal. There hasn’t really been anyone else. 

If I’m being honest, the person I ended up "talking" to the most wasn't a person at all; it was the clinical databases I navigate for work. I found myself late at night, when the house was finally quiet, searching for papers on neonatal immune development. I’d be in the kitchen, tea gone cold, reading about the vaginal microbiome, trying to see if I could "optimize" my own biology before even stepping into the clinic. It felt like I was consulting with a silent, objective ghost rather than a human.

I suppose that surprised me. I’ve always seen myself as someone who values connection, but I’ve built a wall around this part of my life. I haven't even told my closest friends here in Springfield that I’m planning for another. I fear that if I say it out loud to a friend, it becomes "real" in a way that feels permanent, and then I have to explain why I'm so nervous about the care I’ll receive. So, I keep it between me and the data. It’s safer that way. When you're constantly worried about how your status might shift your experience with a provider, you learn to keep your own counsel. Silence feels like a form of protection.

Tell me about your partner's involvement — or the involvement of someone close to you — when you were deciding whether and when to try for a baby. How did those conversations go, and who held the most influence?

I’ve had to learn how to hold my own influence, because there really isn't a partner in the room with me. Being unmarried and navigating life here on my own—well, it means the entire weight of this decision rests on my shoulders alone.

When I look at my children, I feel a fierce, overwhelming drive to grow our family. But that joy is always anchored by the reality of my status. There is no one to turn to and ask, "Are we safe? Will we be okay if things get complicated?" Those are questions you usually ask a partner, someone who shares your life and your legal risks. Instead, I’m the one who has to be the strategist. I’m the one who decides when it’s "safe" enough to try, which mostly means waiting for a window where I feel stable enough to manage the potential, however slight, of a bad interaction at the clinic.

So, the influence? It’s all mine. And that’s a heavy, lonely kind of power. It’s not a conversation; it’s a series of silent calculations. I weigh the desire for a third child against the risk of needing medical care that might expose my vulnerabilities. It’s just me, checking the data, checking my pulse, and trying to decide if the risk of being a "difficult" patient is worth the life I’m trying to bring into this world.
Were there moments when you and your partner — or people close to you — wanted different things? How did you navigate that?

It’s hard to have a disagreement when you’re the only one in the room making the decisions. There isn't a partner here to debate the "ifs" or the "whens" with me. In a way, I think I almost envy those who have someone else to pull them in a different direction—even if they’re arguing, at least they’re sharing the load of the uncertainty. 

For me, the conflict isn't between two people; it’s an internal tug-of-war. One part of me is the mother who just wants to hold a newborn, to experience that joy again, to see my children with a new sibling. That version of me is bold and ready. Then there is the part of me that edits clinical trials, the part that sees the world in cold, hard metrics and knows exactly how much a system can penalize a woman like me for being "non-compliant" or "high-needs."

When I have those moments where I want to pull back, there’s no one to talk me off the ledge. I don’t navigate these fears with a partner; I navigate them by over-preparing. I research until my eyes burn, trying to gain enough "knowledge" to offset the power imbalance I feel at the clinic. It’s a very quiet, very solitary way to handle a life-changing decision. I suppose I’ve just gotten used to being my own only advisor.

Think about the healthcare professionals you saw before you became pregnant — a GP, a fertility specialist, or anyone else. Tell me about those relationships. Who felt most helpful, and who felt more distant?

The people I saw before this pregnancy... it's a complicated memory. My GP is kind, in a very structured, professional way, but there’s always a wall of glass between us. She calls me "low risk," and I know she means it as a comfort—a way of saying, "You don't have to worry." But when she says that, I just feel like a case file being closed. I want to tell her that I’m not just a low-risk profile; I’m a person navigating a very high-stakes reality.

There was a nurse practitioner I saw once for a routine checkup who was the only one who felt... human. She didn’t just look at my chart; she asked how I was balancing work and the kids. For a second, I almost breathed. But then the fear kicked in—that if I admitted I was struggling, or if I asked too many specific questions about my immune profile or the clinic's protocols, she might look at me differently. 

Most of the time, the providers feel distant. They operate with this clinical coldness that I’ve actually written about in my own articles. I recognize the look in their eyes—it’s "the protocol." I know they are just doing their jobs, but it makes me feel like I’m sitting on that crinkly paper as a set of symptoms, not a woman building a family. I’ve learned to play the part of the "ideal patient" because the alternative—being seen as complicated or demanding—is a risk I can't afford to take.

Tell me about the people — online or offline — who you turned to for information and reassurance before you got pregnant. Who was most influential, and how did their advice or stories shape what you believed about pregnancy?

I don’t really turn to people for reassurance. It sounds cold, I know, but "reassurance" feels like a luxury I can't quite trust. Most of the time, I turn to archives. I spend hours in medical journals—the ones I write for and others I access through work. I find a strange, twisted sort of comfort in the raw data. If I can read the meta-analysis on, say, the efficacy of various prenatal supplement regimens, I feel like I’ve exerted some control over the outcome.

There’s this community of professionals online—people who write the studies I read. I don't interact with them, of course, but I follow their work. Their objective, dispassionate tone is the only thing that doesn't trigger my anxiety. If a study says "X causes Y," I don't have to worry about the person’s tone of voice or if they’re looking at me with a judgmental tilt of the head. It’s just information. 

I’ve intentionally avoided the "mommy blogs" or the forums. Those spaces are full of emotional noise, and honestly, they terrify me. People share anecdotes that aren't backed by evidence, and they are so casual about their interactions with doctors. They talk about "pushing back" or "demanding answers." I read those things and my heart rate spikes. I can't be that person. I have to be the woman who knows exactly what she’s doing so that I never have to demand anything. My influence is a textbook, not a person.
Who else was involved in pointing you toward those sources — a friend, a family member, or did you find them yourself?

I found them entirely on my own. My world is very much a self-curated loop of information. I think, in a way, that’s my defense mechanism—if I’m the one hunting down the primary sources and deciphering the clinical language, I’m the one who decides what’s credible. I don't have to worry about someone else’s bias or them pointing me toward an "opinion" that isn't backed by a robust sample size.

It’s isolating, I know. Sometimes, in the middle of a particularly dense paper on maternal health outcomes, I’ll wish someone—just one person—could tell me, "Clemmie, stop reading. You’re doing fine." But I don't have that person. I don't have a friend who would even know where to begin looking, and I wouldn't want to explain why I need to know the specifics of placental vascular resistance, anyway. 

It feels safer to be the gatekeeper of my own knowledge. If I find the source myself, I own the uncertainty. If someone else suggests a source and it turns out to be wrong, or if it leads me down a path that highlights my vulnerabilities as an immigrant in this system, I feel like I’ve lost control. I’d rather be the person who knows everything and trusts no one than the person who trusts the wrong thing and pays the price for it.

Tell me about the women in your family — your mother, grandmothers, sisters, or aunts — and how their experiences of pregnancy and motherhood influenced your own thinking before you even started trying.

My mother and grandmothers… they lived in a world that felt fundamentally different from mine. Back home, pregnancy was something that happened *to* you, a part of the rhythm of life. It was messy, it was loud, and it was deeply communal. They didn't have access to the kind of clinical data I bury myself in, but they had a network of women—a physical, living web of support.

When I look at their experiences, I feel a sharp, stabbing kind of envy. My mother birthed her children with her own mother and sisters in the room, talking, fussing, and grounding her. They didn't worry about being "compliant" or having their legal status scrutinized during a contraction; they were just busy being women. 

But their experiences feel like a different century to me now. If I tried to adopt their approach—the trusting, the letting-go, the reliance on intuition—I think I’d shatter. I can’t afford their vulnerability. I carry the weight of being the first in my family to navigate a high-tech, highly scrutinized American medical system alone. I’ve had to swap their communal wisdom for clinical data because data doesn't judge my passport, and it doesn't leave the room when the paperwork gets complicated. I love them, but their stories of "trusting nature" feel like a beautiful, impossible dream that doesn't fit into the reality of my life in Massachusetts.
How did their stories — whether positive or difficult — show up in your own expectations or fears?

Their stories are like a ghost limb—I can feel where they used to be, but the reality is gone. My mother always spoke about childbirth as something you "just get through," like a storm you weather because the sun is waiting on the other side. She had this unwavering, almost terrifyingly simple faith that the body knows what to do. 

But my fear is that my body isn't enough anymore, or that even if it is, the *system* around me won't let it work. Because I don't have that web of sisters and aunts to buffer me, the "storm" feels much more dangerous. I don't have the luxury of that passive faith. I feel like if I don't keep my eyes on every monitor, every lab result, every policy change in the clinic, I’ll be swept away.

Their stories shaped a fear in me that I’ve had to transform into a science. I’m afraid of being caught off-guard, of being the woman who didn't know enough to save herself when something veered off-track. I look at my mother’s ease and it makes me ache, but I also find myself judging it—thinking, *how could she be so calm?* It’s a sad thing, realizing that my inherited wisdom has been replaced by a diagnostic code. I’ve traded their "trust" for my "control," and I’m still not sure it’s a fair trade.
Tell me about a time — if there was one — when trying to conceive felt harder than you expected. Who was around you during that period, and how did those relationships change or hold steady?

With my first two, I think I was still riding the momentum of optimism. But this time? This time feels different. There was a month last year where I let myself hope, where I tracked every window and felt the physical symptoms of a shift, only for the test to come back negative. I remember sitting in my bathroom, the tiles cold against my legs, looking at the trash can and thinking about how much energy I had put into "optimizing" my environment.

I sat there, completely alone, and it hit me—this isn't just biology. This is a quiet, ongoing interrogation of my own viability. I didn't reach out to anyone. Who would I call? If I called a friend, I’d have to explain why I was crying over a negative test when I already have two healthy children. They’d say, "Clemmie, you're fine, just keep trying." And that would only make me feel more unseen, because they don't understand that for me, this isn't just about a baby. It’s about feeling like I’m building something solid in a place where I’m treated as temporary.

My relationships with everyone stayed exactly the same because I didn't let anyone in. I just went back to my desk, picked up a journal article, and started researching ways to improve implantation rates. I turned the grief into a research project. That’s how I hold steady—by refusing to let the emotion take up space where the data could live instead.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, there’s one thing I don’t think I’ve said out loud, even to myself. Everyone talks about "maternity care" as if it’s just the medical appointments, the blood pressure checks, and the ultrasounds. But for me, it’s also the silence in the waiting room. It’s the way I practice my answers to questions like "Where are you from?" or "What does your husband do?" before I even walk through the clinic doors. 

I think the biggest thing you should know is that for women like me, the "experience" of maternity care isn't confined to the exam room. It’s the labor of existing in two worlds at once. I am constantly editing myself—editing my accent, editing my concerns, editing my own history—so that I can minimize the chance that someone decides I am "other." 

I wish there were a way to tell the doctors that the reason I’m so prepared, the reason I seem so cold or clinical, isn't because I don't care. It’s because I’m scared that if I show them the woman behind the data, the one who is just as terrified and hopeful as any other mother, I won't get the same quality of care. I am working so hard to be the "perfect" patient, but I really just want to be seen as a mother. I just want to know that when I’m at my most vulnerable—when I’m actually in labor—I won't be treated like a statistic or an outlier. I want to be safe. That’s all. It’s a very simple thing to want, but it feels like the hardest thing to ask for.
